worth_it_explanation: Supply Path Optimization (SPO) and reseller hygiene decide how clean the path looks to a buyer when they reach the publisher's inventory through Index Exchange. A path cluttered with redundant resellers, or one that lets made-for-advertising and unauthorized reseller risk sit unmonitored, gives demand-side platforms a reason to cut the path or discount the bid, which lowers the price the inventory clears at. The check is a review of the reseller declarations and path data a publisher already controls, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete declaration or partner the publisher can correct.
intro: SPO is the process buyers use to favor the cleanest, most direct route to a publisher's inventory and to drop the paths that add intermediaries without adding value. For a publisher selling through Index Exchange, the audit question is whether the paths to their inventory read as clean to a buyer, which depends on the reseller declarations in ads.txt and sellers.json and on how unauthorized reseller and made-for-advertising (MFA) risk is monitored. These checks confirm that redundant or low-value reseller paths are minimized and that MFA and unauthorized reseller exposure is watched rather than left to accumulate.

audit_checks.title: Redundant or low-value reseller paths to the same inventory are not minimized
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the same impression reaches a buyer through several reseller paths, Supply Path Optimization tends to favor the cleanest route and discount or drop the rest, so duplicate paths add intermediary fees without adding demand. A publisher who leaves redundant resellers declared dilutes the bid on inventory that a single direct path through Index Exchange could carry at a higher clearing price.

educate.base:
When a buyer evaluates a bid request, they can see every path that leads to the impression. Supply Path Optimization (SPO) is the buyer's practice of choosing the path that reaches the inventory most directly and dropping paths that add an intermediary without adding demand. The standards that make this visible are ads.txt, which a publisher posts to declare who is authorized to sell their inventory, and sellers.json, which an exchange such as Index Exchange (IX) posts to declare the sellers and resellers it represents. Read together, they let a buyer trace each path from their own platform back to the publisher.

The problem a publisher can create for themselves is declaring the same inventory through more reseller paths than the inventory needs. Every reseller in a path takes a margin, so a path that runs through two or three intermediaries clears at a lower net price than the same impression sold through one direct seat. When a buyer sees the identical impression arrive through a clean direct path and through a longer reseller path at the same time, SPO favors the clean path and discounts or stops bidding on the longer one. The redundant paths do not add demand. They add fees and they give the buyer a reason to question the supply.

Reseller declarations accumulate over time. A reseller relationship that made sense when it was set up can outlive its value as direct connections replace it, yet the 'RESELLER' line stays in ads.txt and the seller stays in the sellers.json chain. The audit question is whether the reseller paths that remain are still carrying demand the direct path cannot, or whether they are duplicating a route the publisher already has through Index Exchange.

A few signs point to reseller paths that are not minimized:
  • The same inventory is declared through multiple resellers in ads.txt that all resolve to the same demand, with no direct path carrying more of it
  • Reporting shows reseller paths clearing at a lower net price than the direct Index Exchange path for comparable impressions
  • Reseller lines remain in ads.txt for partners the publisher no longer transacts with or cannot name a reason to keep
You may want to confirm that each reseller path still earns its place by carrying demand the direct path does not. One approach is to compare net clearing price and unique demand across the direct path and each reseller path, then remove the declarations that only duplicate a route you already hold.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, reseller relationships often build up across years of monetization changes, so a publisher may carry resellers from a former ad-management vendor alongside their current direct Index Exchange seat. You may want to trace each reseller path back to the demand it brings and remove the ones that only duplicate the direct path, so buyers see one clean route to the premium inventory rather than several competing ones.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, the display inventory may be a smaller share of revenue, so reseller paths can sit unreviewed for long stretches. You may want to read which reseller paths actually carry demand for the commercial inventory and drop the redundant ones, so the cleaner path keeps more of each impression's value rather than splitting it across intermediaries.

learn_more.source_title: How to optimize header bidding setup

investigate.intro: Review the reseller declarations in ads.txt and the seller chain in sellers.json against the paths that actually carry demand, and look for reseller paths that duplicate the direct Index Exchange route.

ads_txt.label: Publisher ads.txt file
steps: Open the publisher's '/ads.txt' file on the root domain and list every line, noting which are marked 'DIRECT' and which are marked 'RESELLER'.
For each 'RESELLER' line, identify the exchange and seller account it points to and whether the same inventory is also reachable through a 'DIRECT' line.
Record any reseller the publisher cannot name a current reason to keep, and any case where several resellers resolve to the same demand.

sellers_json.label: Index Exchange sellers.json
steps: Open Index Exchange's public sellers.json file and locate the seller entries that match the account identifiers in the publisher's ads.txt.
Confirm each declared seller is the entity the publisher expects and that the seller_type (e.g., 'PUBLISHER' or 'INTERMEDIARY') matches the relationship.
Note any reseller path that adds an intermediary between the publisher and Index Exchange where a direct seat already exists.

ix_reporting.label: Index Exchange reporting
steps: Build a report that breaks revenue and average clearing price out by the path or seat the demand arrived through.
Compare the net price of the direct Index Exchange path against each reseller path for comparable inventory.
Record which reseller paths clear at a lower net price or bring no demand the direct path lacks, which marks them as candidates to remove.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each reseller path to the inventory, the demand it carries, and its net clearing price against the direct Index Exchange path for comparable impressions. A setup where every reseller path carries demand the direct path does not, at a net price that justifies the extra intermediary, passes. A setup where reseller paths duplicate the direct route, clear at a lower net price, or remain declared for partners the publisher no longer transacts with is a fail recorded with the specific paths and declarations affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each reseller path, the demand it carries, and its net clearing price against the direct path above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} declares the same inventory through reseller paths that duplicate the direct Index Exchange (IX) route without carrying demand the direct path lacks. Supply Path Optimization (SPO) leads buyers to favor the cleanest route and discount or drop redundant paths, so each extra reseller adds a margin and a reason for the buyer to question the supply rather than adding demand. {{voice_recommend}} comparing net clearing price and unique demand across the direct path and each reseller path, then removing the ads.txt and sellers.json declarations that only duplicate a route {{site_name}} already holds. After the change, recheck which paths carry demand to confirm the remaining resellers still earn their place.

audit_checks.title: Made-for-advertising and unauthorized reseller risk is not monitored
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Unauthorized resellers selling a publisher's inventory through paths the publisher never declared expose the supply to spoofing and to buyer blocklists, and made-for-advertising association can pull a clean publisher's bids down with it. Left unmonitored, both can cut demand across the whole account at once, which makes this a higher-stakes hygiene gap than a single redundant path.

educate.base:
Two related risks sit on the supply path beyond the publisher's own declarations. The first is the unauthorized reseller, an entity selling the publisher's inventory through a path the publisher never authorized in ads.txt. A buyer who traces that path back and finds no matching 'RESELLER' line treats the supply as spoofed, and the response is often to block the path or distrust the domain. The second is made-for-advertising (MFA) association, where a publisher's inventory shares demand paths or partners with sites built mainly to carry ads rather than to serve readers. Buyers increasingly screen MFA out of their supply, and a clean publisher who shares a reseller with MFA inventory can see their own bids pulled down by the company they keep.

The standards that expose these risks are the same ones that enable Supply Path Optimization. ads.txt declares who the publisher authorizes, sellers.json declares who an exchange such as Index Exchange (IX) represents, and the OpenRTB SupplyChain object records every hop a bid request took. When a publisher monitors these together, an unauthorized path shows up as a seller selling their domain with no authorizing ads.txt line, and an MFA-adjacent path shows up as a reseller the publisher shares with inventory they would not want to be grouped with.

The gap this check looks for is the absence of monitoring, not the presence of a single bad actor. Supply paths change without the publisher acting. Resellers can add a domain to their own declarations, and a shared partner can take on MFA inventory after the relationship was set up. A publisher who checks their declarations once at setup and never again has no way to see an unauthorized path appear or an MFA association form. The audit question is whether the publisher watches their declared paths against the paths buyers actually see, on a schedule, rather than assuming the setup holds.

A few signs point to MFA and unauthorized reseller risk going unmonitored:
  • No routine compares the publisher's ads.txt and the Index Exchange sellers.json against the seller paths buyers report seeing for the domain
  • The publisher cannot say when they last checked for sellers declaring their inventory without an authorizing ads.txt line
  • Reseller partners are not reviewed for the other inventory they carry, so an MFA association could form unnoticed
You may want to confirm that the publisher watches their supply paths on a schedule rather than only at setup. One approach is to reconcile the declared paths against the paths buyers see at a set interval, and to review each reseller for the company its other inventory keeps.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, the domain itself carries brand value, so an unauthorized reseller spoofing the domain or a made-for-advertising association can damage buyer trust beyond the lost revenue on a single path. You may want to reconcile declared paths against the paths buyers see on a regular schedule and review reseller partners for the other inventory they carry, so the premium domain is not grouped with supply that drags its price down.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, display monetization may run alongside the core commerce business, so an unauthorized path or a made-for-advertising association can sit unnoticed while attention stays on the store. You may want to set a recurring check that compares the declared sellers against the paths buyers report for the domain, so a reseller adding the inventory without authorization is caught before it affects demand.

investigate.intro: Review whether the publisher reconciles their declared sellers against the paths buyers actually see, and whether reseller partners are checked for unauthorized declarations and made-for-advertising association on a schedule.

ads_txt_sellers_json.label: ads.txt and sellers.json reconciliation
steps: List the authorized sellers and resellers in the publisher's '/ads.txt' file and the matching entries in Index Exchange's sellers.json.
Search public sellers.json files of other exchanges for the publisher's domain to find any seller declaring the inventory without a matching authorizing line in ads.txt.
Record any unauthorized path found and note whether the publisher has a routine that would have caught it.

supply_chain_object.label: SupplyChain object review
steps: Where the publisher can see the OpenRTB SupplyChain object for their bid requests, trace the hops each request records from Index Exchange back to the publisher.
Confirm each hop matches an authorized seller and that no unexpected intermediary appears in the chain.
Note any reseller in the chain that also carries inventory the publisher would not want to be grouped with, which marks a made-for-advertising association to review.

monitoring_routine.label: Monitoring schedule review
steps: Ask the publisher when they last reconciled their declared paths against the paths buyers report and whether the check is scheduled or one-off.
Ask whether reseller partners are reviewed for the other inventory they carry and how often.
Record whether a recurring monitoring routine exists or whether the declarations were checked only at setup.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a recurring routine reconciles the publisher's authorized sellers against the paths buyers see, whether unauthorized declarations were found, and whether reseller partners are reviewed for made-for-advertising association. A setup with a scheduled reconciliation, no open unauthorized paths, and a reseller-review practice passes. A setup where paths were checked only at setup, where an unauthorized seller is declaring the inventory, or where reseller partners are never reviewed is a fail recorded with the unmonitored paths and partners affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a recurring reconciliation exists, any unauthorized paths found, and whether resellers are reviewed for made-for-advertising association above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not monitor its supply paths for unauthorized resellers or made-for-advertising (MFA) association beyond the initial setup. An unauthorized seller declaring the inventory reads as spoofed supply and invites buyer blocklists, and an MFA association through a shared reseller can pull clean bids down, so both can cut demand across the account when left unwatched. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling the authorized sellers in ads.txt and the Index Exchange (IX) sellers.json against the paths buyers actually see on a set schedule, and reviewing each reseller partner for the other inventory it carries. After establishing the routine, recheck the declared paths at the chosen interval so an unauthorized path or a new MFA association is caught early.
